speaker: interviewer
text: These arms that you purchase, the guns, the machine guns, what is it that you do with them? How do you use them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We bring them to the ghetto, first of all, or they are delivered, delivered by the Poles, which I am buying, to the wall, or to the cemetery, or somehow to the ghetto. Canals to the ghetto, if that's a big bundle, big-- which I like to pay for it to be delivered. Or small things we're taking in ourself together, if we knew for sure that we bribed the, the, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Guard.

speaker: interviewee
text: The guards. But that was a flying guard. That mean, two SS officers, very low-ranking officers, which came in with a bike, a motorbike, or a bikes, and check on the spot what is happening. A, a, a flying-- a flying--

speaker: interviewer
text: A check-spot, virtually.

speaker: interviewee
text: Check-spot. That was death penalty.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was death penalty.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because they always found something. If ammunition and things, the whole lot is shot. But they always found-- smuggled food or things, and they shot for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were shot for smuggling food, as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: For smuggling food, for having money with you, yes. On the spot. And we did have one like that, where, unfortunately, my wife was witnessing, and myself, witnessing a young boy was shot for 40 zloty, which was zero.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any-- you had no way of signaling that this spot check was on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't know. This we didn't know. Frankelstein and [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So having--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you who that was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Having brought the guns into the ghetto, what, what was the significance of them? What did you plan to do with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Having brought the guns into the ghetto, what was the-- what was their use or purpose?

speaker: interviewee
text: They being stored for the-- for the time when that will come to use them. And that being kept for us in case of an attack or something from the-- and that was kept by us by collecting money from the richer Jews. Where we come with guns, or to get rid of, of, of our enemy inside the ghetto at the moment. The cleaning of the police, or the cleaning of the people which did work with the Gestapo. Yes. We used the guns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you manage to bring guns in every day? Or was that just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no no. Not every day. If we got them, if we could get them at that time-- no, not every day. We went out to get them, and we couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: David, how-- approximately how many Jews were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto? About-- in-- at the-- at the height of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: At the beginning, and then, now, we're talking a year later.

speaker: interviewee
text: In my book, was from 400,000 to 500,000, approximately. 350,000 to 500,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: So David--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the highest point.

speaker: interviewer
text: You brought in five guns at a time. For three--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not five guns at a time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. One at a time.

speaker: interviewer
text: One at a time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So with your stockpile of guns being increased at one at a time, how could they have any effect on the lives of 500,000 people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I am not only one buying it. There's more people buying it. I am not the only one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please expand on that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not the only one buying it. And I did another ways of buying from the organization, or getting from the organization outside, which I got no connection with it. I got nothing to-- because you will find in Ringelblum's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this Ringelblum?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ringelblum's book, you'll find that we been the most, the most, as from all organizations, having guns, machine guns, and uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Emanuel Ringelblum. We're talking about the historian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. His own-- his own writing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: He give you a full-- a full-- it says it was the place which was the most--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you store these guns, David?

speaker: interviewee
text: We'd been storing them, hiding them in the cellars. But then when was the cleaning of the Jews, when they send out the 300,000, and we'd been left 60,000, we opened simply on a ground floor, and first floor, in a wide area, where nobody's coming. Only the Germans can come, and the Polish, to clean our places. We did [INAUDIBLE] our, our our stockroom, in our offices, in our bunker,

speaker: interviewer
text: Your center point.

speaker: interviewee
text: In our bunker, open completely. And the saying was that so ever will come in won't get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: If they were not Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: If they are not from the organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this place?

speaker: interviewee
text: 7 9-- two numbers-- Muranowska Street.

speaker: interviewer
text: 7 to 9 Muranowska Street.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because that was two numbers in the street, in this number. Because that was a corner place. So 7 was the one Muranowska, and then the next Muranowska, 9, was Platz Muranowska. That was a place where I actually brought in Karski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jan Karski.

speaker: interviewee
text: Professor Karski. I brought in [INAUDIBLE]. I brought in, through from Muranowska 6, the Christian side, to Muranowska 7, our Jewish side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's try and clarify this, David. So you said that this building in Muranowska Street, was in the wild section of the ghetto. What did you mean by that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wild mean that was cleaned up by the Germans. That's no more the Jews are living there. In smaller ghettos, closed in, when they made the cleaning-- the cleaning in Mila Street, when they took out--

speaker: interviewer
text: 18 Mila.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not 18 Mila.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not 18 Mila.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Mila Street.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mila Street was a cleaning--

speaker: interviewer
text: M-I-L-A.

speaker: interviewee
text: --before, three months before the uprising was a clean three, four months, and they took all the Jews to sort out the ones which are working. And they left in Warsaw about 40,000 in their opinion. In our opinion, 60,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did-- in this cleaned ghetto area-- how did you, ZZW, move backwards and forwards without being detected?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did, except that I got some people left over from the ones which I select. We moved quietly. We moved from room-- from house to house. We did have openings in every house, from one house-- we could move streets, not to be seen--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're suggeesting--

speaker: interviewee
text: --but from one street across the street, we did have to go over the top.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you suggest that you've got-- you could move from house to house, did you have holes in the wall to move through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Holes in the cellars. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And through the cellars.

speaker: interviewee
text: We could move streets, full streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you say cellars, then you're suggesting under the ground you had a network within the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not under the ground. In the cellars. Because every house did have a cellar.

speaker: interviewer
text: But a cellar is below ground level.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you could move--

speaker: interviewee
text: From one cellar to the other one, from one house to the other house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you could remove--

speaker: interviewee
text: We open it.

speaker: interviewer
text: --completely from one street to the other without detection.

speaker: interviewee
text: Completely. But when we got to cross a street-- the, the, the, the, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The road.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the road, we did have to go out of the house and cross the road to the next--

speaker: interviewer
text: Openly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Across the road. Across the road. To same street, but across the road. If you want to cross, we got to go to the normal street. We smuggled. We've seen, if it's clean, if nobody's there, we ran across. That was a risk, yes, for sure. Everything was a risk.

speaker: interviewer
text: David, you mentioned Jan Karski. Could you explain Jan Karski and the context that you got to know him, please? And could you please identify the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. 1942. August. End of August.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you-- what connection did you have with Jan Karski?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd been asked by the chiefs of our organization. That was Rodel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rodel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Rodel. Which said to me that we got to bring in a Christian here. No name. No, he didn't even said a Christian. He said, two people to bring into the ghetto. And two of you boys will go out and be the guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: The guide.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: The guide.

speaker: interviewee
text: The guard, guarding--

speaker: interviewer
text: Guard. Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --those two. They are very important people, and you will bring them in. We went out, and [INAUDIBLE] corroborated, you will find in the book of [INAUDIBLE], that he came in. But then he will write to you, that's one of the leaders of the organization, Bund, was taking him in the ghetto. He is not writing anything about bringing in. No one book you'll find that you have brought in. He didn't flew, did he, to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. So how was he brought in, and how do you know about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I want to tell you, how he was brought in. The most saved, safe tunnel was built by us, by--

speaker: interviewer
text: By ZZW.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, by coincidence or not coincidence, that I don't want to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it built?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was built in 19-- beginning 1942. That was built-- that's some versions-- that was built by engineers and by this. We didn't have any. That was built by young boys about 12, 13 year old. Because if people are intelligent enough to understand it, that to build a meter by about 3/4 of a meter. You can't if old people, big people there. You've got to have kids to do it, because they're small.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this tunnel built from, to?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Muranowska 6, Christian side, to Muranowska 7 9, underground. We came across-- when we build it, we came across a pipe. This pipe is very important to know about it. A, a, a sewer pipe. And we couldn't go over, because we'll be too close to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Surface.

speaker: interviewee
text: Surface. And we got to go under. So made a step down for the pipe. And it's very important to know about the pipe. I'll tell you in a minute why. We'd been sent out by the top two boys. Across the street was only about five, six houses where one side could see a street about five kilometers, and on another side, about five kilometers. You could see. So we are standing on both sides. We got a gun. We got a machine gun, and we got a revolver.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is one guard on one side, and another one on another. They didn't said which way they will come. This street or that street. That's all security. But, boys, if you will see him-- they described them quite well. And there wasn't a big traffic, because near a wall-- because in the middle of the street of Muranowska between 7 and between 6 was a wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: A ghetto wall.

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto wall. So there, if the Poles even come home, they rushed. They didn't want to be near the wall, because something can happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. There was a fear.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, that you could see immediately who is who. So if you will see the two people, one will be Jewish-looking-- that's Karski. And one would be Polish-looking. That's a Jew. Big mustaches, big mustaches, yes. The Jewish fellow didn't look exactly like him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that they, they looked the opposite to what the were.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because they told, we told, to dress themselves as Jewish as you can. Because they're Christian [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Fine, fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they went to the-- they came through my side. What I've been told through all this is to step a little bit further in front of them, and show them the way. That's in the book described. In Karski's book. And to go slowly, in slowly, before, in front of them. And when I start to turn the corner, I went to the entrance of the building.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of 6.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of number 7.

speaker: interviewer
text: 7 On the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not number 6.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: 6. And then I went and stopped there. When they came closer, I went inside the building. When they came closer, and that's why I took him down to the canal. Now, and one of the Poles is giving a statement that she knew about it. And to prove that she knew about it, she's telling, saying, that a carpet was lying in the cellar, covering the entrance. She didn't seen it. She doesn't know about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because I won't claim that was different just saying that I know different. But I will ask any intelligent person to know, and to prove, to think of it, how could be a carpet lying in a cellar, on a dirt floor? That's nonsense. And that wouldn't be done that way. That wasn't done that way. So how was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was covered magnificently. That was a water, soda-water factory. And was a big thing there for the gas what that used to fill in to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: To put the bubbles through.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to put the bubbles. That--

speaker: interviewer
text: Carbonate.

speaker: interviewee
text: That one we could lift up. We could bring him up. That was where I worked there, to get the bubbles, you know, the chemical in. And we could hook it on, and lift it up. We made it to lift up. Underneath of it was a tunnel which went there to us, but was about a foot of water in the-- in the tunnel all the time. Not in the tunnel, but in the entrance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: For dogs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So dogs could-- the smell could be eliminated.

speaker: interviewee
text: With water, with this, they can't smell.

speaker: interviewer
text: How can you-- once you were in the tunnel, coming from the Jewish side to the Aryan side, how could you lift-- you could still lift this cabinet?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We arranged it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You arranged for somebody to lift only one way.

speaker: interviewee
text: We arranged someone to be outside and let us in. The boy in the-- that's mine, mine observation, but I didn't prove it. And I haven't got it proved. Mine information-- mine observation was that the Jewish boy, which was the Kazik, Kazik, only the information which I set to the museum in Israel, and when I said Kazik, one of the people which know about the happening there, which he wrote a book about it, said, I think that you are right. That was the name, Kazik, because we know another name. But that was Kazik. We know now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did Kazik do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kazik was a young boy, and the story goes that his father and mother have been taken to Ravensbruck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ravensbruck, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, everybody was taken from Poles, and killed in Ravensbruck. And he's the son of the people. And he got-- he's the janitor of this building.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of Number 6?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of Number 6. And the beauty of it was that the Poles there didn't know the janitor before, because that was a Jewish place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a Christian place. And the Poles came in when the Jews had been thrown of Muranowska. So this story went out beautifully. That's strictly mine opinion that he was Jewish. I must say that I didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: You never had it confirmed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't have any proof of it, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was the one watching everything. Now Karski came in there. I brought Karski in. And I was the first one to walk, and in the back was another boy to walk, to get him in to the other side. When we brought him inside, he hit his head on the pipe, and he said a very, very bad Polish swearing word. And he said that he didn't have to bow that low even in the church or something, like he did have to bow his head here. A very [INAUDIBLE]. Then he came out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- Yeah, on the Jewish side.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we came out, number 7 9. And two people have been waiting there. ZOB.

speaker: interviewer
text: The German?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, ZOB. ZOB. [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. ZOB.

speaker: interviewee
text: From our opposition.

speaker: interviewer
text: Z-O-B. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The two boys to take them out of there. They told us their secret name, their secret there. That was a secret word, and we get out with them. And we start to walk with him together, to the next house what I brought him in, Karski. Now there's a very important thing. How Karski knew, after so many years, that David was the one, or David know Karski was the one. That's very important. And he came to a home, he got his, his talk with the-- with the leaders. And then-- we didn't, we haven't been, when they talk between.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they were speaking.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were secret talks.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: David, just before we do that, what was the purpose of Jan Karski coming into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was actually asked by the Polish underground that the Jews want him, because before he's going overseas on a mission to England and America, to, to try to give a message from the Jews. And this is your own will, because I spoke to Karski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of his own free will.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is on your own will. If you'd like to go into the ghetto, we'll let you in. We'll let you go. They will arrange it, we'll let you go. But if you don't want it, you can refuse it. because it's risky.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he chose to go into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was a very good-- look, he-- to me, he's absolutely God. What he did for us, nobody would do it. He came in there, and, as exactly, he was dressed like a Jew. And he came in, and went up there, and this is the most important few things which happened there. First of all, I saw him at the window. When he seen two boys, German shooting to a window, and killing some people. And he nearly collapsed. And a woman told him, you better go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was inside the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside the ghetto already, inside the room what I used to have the conference. And he was very nervous about it. And then, this is the most important thing, which nobody knew about it. I did give it to the book, to his book. He, because he's so delicate, he's so-- such a-- such a gentleman, such a nice man, that he didn't even want to talk about it. He was offered a glass of coffee, which looks like coffee, and a piece of bread with marmalade. He pushed away the bread, and he drank the coffee. After we knew well, in the ghetto, we start to talk about it. We knew well why he pushed away the piece of bread. Piece of bread, you might think, that's one piece of bread. Can't save a life. Yes, that can save a life. One simple piece of bread can save your life for the next three months. One piece.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how come? After this piece of bread, either you find a bread, or you find some money, or you get something else, and that will take you over that you die. Continuous.

speaker: interviewee
text: When you go to die. But you haven't got a piece of bread. He understood it. And he didn't accept the bread. He was sitting here, not far from you, in my place, and tears went down his--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were streaming down.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I told him, why didn't you tell in your book. But I did have to bring it to your book. Because I got a book--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his answer?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I thank me for the informations I did give him. [INAUDIBLE]. So we've been sitting here, and we start to talk. He said, look, David. And then, he's coming out. He said, look, David. I want to tell you something. The faces, you, me, we can't remember. That's, that's, yes. But I tell you something. Does not exist anybody in the world to know those details without being with me. And I, mine sight is [INAUDIBLE]. There's not a man, another one than me, to remember your movements there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: So you are the David.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was identification of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said it in-- on a tape in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when Jan Karski-- when you took him back, what message did he take out, and to whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- I-- I know after. I didn't know before.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't know at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: But at the time, I-- no, no, I didn't. He took out the message, and he went to England, and to the war ministry. And he spoke to Eden. He spoke with Roosevelt. Unfortunately, nobody could believe. That's what I say, always. If I got to speak to someone, or I'm speaking to some boys, to an organization, I am starting go to private people. I'm starting of ladies and gentleman, you won't offend me not believing my stories. I don't believe, myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: David, how-- was-- did Jan Karski only come into the ghetto one time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Apparently it was twice, but I don't know about  the second.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know about the second time.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know before or after. I never asked.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have a question that's puzzling me. When you came out on the Aryan side on number 6 Muranowska Street, how come, with the comings and going in an empty house, there was no detection that it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no, no, no, no. The coming of the-- of the empty house-- as I told you, when you're coming to the cellar of 7 9, I can take you to [INAUDIBLE], three kilometers away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. But on the Aryan side, how come it wasn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was a, a living house. People lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: People were living there. OK. So what other activities-- you said you were married. Did you spend much time at home, or were you more in interaction with the ZZW?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spent quite a time, because night times, I-- or if I went out, I went out for two or three days. No, I spent quite a bit with Luba.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your family at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: At this stage, all over the places.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not all in one house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in contact with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I've been in contact. Very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other--

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been busy with my work, and with my wife already.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other day-to-day activities conducted your life in the ghetto? Were you aware when an action was taking place of cleaning the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got-- we got a tip from always from the organization, that's tomorrow it will be not too, not too good time. Be careful. Don't walk. Don't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Juden--

speaker: interviewee
text: We did have tips from the Christian side-- not me, which had have the tips, but the, the top brass, our brass, our top, they knew about. Not always, but they knew when it was something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Judenrat have to select names, or did they just have to select numbers when they were asked--

speaker: interviewee
text: Numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Numbers. So that they-- like one, two, three, how was it done? When they had to select a number of people for deportation, how was it done?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took the poorest one.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took the poorest one?

speaker: interviewee
text: The poorest one, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a very--

speaker: interviewee
text: The poorest one had been taken first, because they went for the three pieces of bread, or three breads, with a little bit of marmalade.

speaker: interviewer
text: So wasn't that a--

speaker: interviewee
text: They've been given by the Germans, if they go voluntarily, three piece, three breads, three kilo bread, and some marmalade for the bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the people that turned up to collect that--

speaker: interviewee
text: They been-- they have to go up to the-- to the-- what you call it? To the wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, to the train. To the train station.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. To the Umschlagplatz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: There they are--

speaker: interviewer
text: The assembly place.

speaker: interviewee
text: --getting three pieces, three kilo bread, and marmalade. So if you got two children with them, is getting nine bread. That's, that's rotten. In the train. But still, he's getting it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was-- so--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was given.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. That was given. And then they were put on the train. But how come these people went with their families? Did they have to show that they were-- actually did own-- did have children that were with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they wouldn't get the bread--

speaker: interviewer
text: On their own.

speaker: interviewee
text: On their own, because they get, and get ready when the go to the Umschlagplatz. But they're already locked. Closed. So they can't give the breads back to the family, and go for Kiddush HaShem. No, they can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you say they were actually locked? The assembly place is not the wagons. So you said, how could they--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they are going to the Umschlagplatz, which is locked. The Umschlagplatz is a place--

speaker: interviewer
text: A square.

speaker: interviewee
text: --where is, a square, where is blocked with the high walls, and a gate, and when you go there from there, you can't get out. With, with, with Ukrainians, and Lithuanians, and they're watching inside, and killing the people already before they go to the train. They are killed. On the spot.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was not the first time there was an assembly, so wasn't word-- wasn't there word around the ghetto that this is what would happen, and the people still went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We been telling the people, from the very beginning, what we-- when we knew about Treblinka, end of 1941, beginning '42. But the, the older people, and the religious people, said to us, that we are bandits, not liberators. You are bandits, and this is a sin to kill, and to go around killing. We shouldn't do it. Because we are only making the Germans nervous. They kill all of us. We tried to explain. We tried to say we've been bandits and murderers in the eyes of the older Jews. Older Jews, I say from 47, 48. That's to me old at that time. They didn't let us do--

speaker: interviewer
text: From 45, 47 years age.

speaker: interviewee
text: Age, up. They didn't let us do anything. They didn't want us to do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: David, the Judenrat was supposed to be the direction of the ghetto. And yourselves, ZZW and ZOB, were the soldiers, the partisans, or whatever the case may be. You were very few in numbers. How much power did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did have in our-- first of all, we did have, in mine opinion, what I got from some books, and from a little bit of knowledge from inside, I think we been about 400 plus. The ZOB was about 800 plus. Double. The ZOB was the strongest organization in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't possibly fight, or conduct actions the whole time against the Germans. So when, in so-called, in inverted commas, 'normal' times, what tasks did you perform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cleaning up with the people which are working for the Gestapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cleaning up. Do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cleaning, that's mean killing the people work with the Gestapo. Taking notes, and preparing ourself for a final, for a final fight with the Germans. When that will come, we didn't know at the beginning. But we are prepared of anything it will take the Germans with their connection to clean up the ghetto. That mean we got to get out. We got to get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in training at-- during this time, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it that you were trained?

speaker: interviewee
text: We've been training always in the cellars. We never used real bullets, never. Only holding the gun, or holding the machine gun, and training and telling us how and what to do. We been trained, mostly, if the killing have to come, knifes, wires, and bayonets. And, in some cases, the helmet which is wearing on. We been trained how to kill him with his own helmet. When you pull a helmet back from his head, from the back, the band around is choking you to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: To death. And all of you felt confident that you were able to perform these actions if the need arose?

speaker: interviewee
text: No man is-- you see, I was a boy of 19, 20, 21, 22-- I'd say young people were not taking the way I'm thinking now. Because now, in my opinion, I would like to see some danger things, how to survive, not how to fight. But at that time, no. My enemy, mine-- my thig what happened to my people, the Germans, I crossed off a matter of fact after once writing Nazis. I'm not calling it Nazis. I'm calling Germans. To me, the word Nazis is nonexistent, practically. I got it in my speech, the first run, because, I, in my book, the whole German nation is guilty. Not Nazis. I can't see any Nazis, because the, the, the thing which happen to Hitler in the end of the war was only one, and the end of the war, not one German went against it. I haven't seen him. I haven't heard about them. To me, those are Germans, not Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were not Germans that were controlled by another person? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You feel these-- the Germans, the population--

speaker: interviewee
text: As Germans, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --could have said no?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, look. What someone would said no, one or two, whatever-- no, but I seen, I, I have heard, that Stroop, Stroop, which was liquidizing the ghetto, took something, and he got in his report that they did have there Wehrmacht fighting the Jews in the ghetto. So Wehrmacht, which was always, always I've been told it's a beautiful-- that never goes against the Jews. Again, they've been fighting against the Jews, too. Killing the Jews, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So to me, that's the nation there, the whole lot. I seen the films. I seen the hand raised on, on, on, on the-- on these meetings. All Germans. Civilians. OK, David.
